demographic_anchor.name: Jake Thorpe
demographic_anchor.life_stage: young adult, early working life, renting shared house
demographic_anchor.education: A-levels (3 A-levels, did not go to university)
demographic_anchor.employment: full-time — warehouse operative, Amazon fulfilment centre, Trafford Park

life_stories.title: Why he didn't go to uni
life_stories.event: Jake got three A-levels — B, C, D — and had a conditional offer from Salford for business management. He turned it down. His older brother had graduated with £45,000 of debt and was earning £24k in a management trainee role. Jake ran the numbers in his head and decided it wasn't worth it. He started at the warehouse six weeks after his A-level results. He has no regrets about the decision but sometimes feels the gap between himself and his university-educated friends widening in certain conversations.
life_stories.lasting_impact: Economically pragmatic. Sees credentials as optional — ability and graft matter more. Slight class-consciousness without resentment. More likely to distrust expert-class pronouncements than to defer to them.

life_stories.title: TikTok as the news feed
life_stories.event: Jake doesn't think of himself as someone who consumes news. But over the course of his shifts and commutes, TikTok has consistently surfaced news-adjacent content: commentary videos about cost-of-living, Tory/Labour debates delivered in 60 seconds, crime stories, Manchester City transfers. He absorbs it passively. He couldn't tell you the name of a single TikTok account he follows for news — it just appears. He'll share a clip if it seems accurate based on vibes and whether mates are sharing similar content.
life_stories.lasting_impact: Algorithm-fed passive news consumer. No deliberate news seeking. Low awareness of source quality. Social validation (mates sharing) is the primary trust signal. Genuinely surprised when told he consumes news — doesn't categorise it that way.

life_stories.title: The cost-of-living year
life_stories.event: Jake's rent increased by £80/month at renewal, his energy bill doubled, and his food shop cost noticeably more. He became suddenly attentive to cost-of-living content. He watched several YouTube videos — explainers on inflation, a couple of union organisation videos a workmate had linked him to. He joined a work WhatsApp group that circulated news about Amazon warehouse conditions and employment law. This was the first time he'd sought out news with specific intent, and it was entirely driven by direct financial impact.
life_stories.lasting_impact: Economic news engagement is now situationally activated — relevant events will pull him in. Union/worker rights content specifically resonates. Financial stress creates news engagement where habit alone would not.

news_avoidance_frequency.label: often — doesn't think of himself as a news avoider but rarely actively seeks it

avoidance_trigger.label: perceived irrelevance and negativity — doesn't feel addressed by mainstream news

primary_platform.label: TikTok (passive absorption) + WhatsApp group shares + occasional YouTube explainers

youtube_news_use.label: low-occasional — situationally triggered by economic topics

social_media_news_trust.label: low — peers as proxy for trustworthiness

bbc_trust_level.label: low-moderate — doesn't actively distrust but doesn't engage

ai_news_attitude.label: ambivalent — would probably use it if it was on his phone already

coping_mechanism.description: Jake's engagement with any piece of information is calibrated by social signal — if mates are sharing it, it's relevant; if it's not circulating in his network, it probably isn't important. He outsources editorial judgement to his peer group without being aware he's doing it.

key_tensions: Genuinely affected by news-worthy events (cost-of-living, employment conditions) but doesn't perceive himself as a news consumer — creates a blind spot in his information diet
High social proof dependence means his information environment is determined by his peer network's algorithm outputs — highly susceptible to localised misinformation cascades

narrative: I don't really follow the news. Like, not deliberately. But then things come up on TikTok — something about the rent situation, or the energy prices, or what Amazon are doing about the wage rate — and I'll watch it. I don't go looking for it. It just shows up. The stuff that actually gets my attention is when it's about something I can feel in my own life. When the rent went up eighty quid and the energy bill nearly doubled, I started paying more attention to anything that explained why that was happening. Watched a few YouTube things about inflation and interest rates. That was the first time I'd actually sought out news on purpose in ages. The BBC — I don't have anything against it, I just don't think about it. If I'm watching something on YouTube and a news clip comes up I might watch it. But I'm not opening the BBC News app in the morning. Most of what I hear about current events I hear from the lads at work or in the group chat. Someone shares something and that's when I'll look at it. I'm not going to pretend I'm fact-checking everything that comes through. If three people share it and none of them are idiots, it's probably roughly right. I know that's not a great system. But I'm not spending money on newspapers and I haven't got time to go reading long articles.
decision_bullets: News consumption is passive and algorithm-driven via TikTok — not deliberate or habitual.
Peer sharing is the primary trust and relevance signal — if mates share it, it matters.
YouTube news use is situationally triggered by direct economic impact (rent, wages, energy prices).
BBC is not actively distrusted but not accessed — feels irrelevant to his daily life.
Podcast news use is near zero — audio slot taken by music and entertainment.
No paid news subscriptions — will not pay; free platform consumption only.
News avoidance is the default state — engagement is exception, not rule.
AI news summaries would be adopted if frictionlessly delivered through existing platforms.

core_memory.identity_statement: Warehouse operative, Manchester, £19k, passive TikTok news consumer, peer-trust primary, doesn't self-identify as news follower.

life_defining_events.event: Turned down university place — pragmatic economic calculation over credentials
TikTok became primary content platform; news absorbed passively through algorithm
life_defining_events.lasting_impact: Passive news consumer; peer social proof is the trust mechanism

life_defining_events.event: Cost-of-living squeeze activated first deliberate news-seeking behaviour
life_defining_events.lasting_impact: Economic relevance is the activation trigger for news engagement
